Using Graphic Organizers
Flow chart 1, 2
Use a ﬂow chart to show the 
sequence of events or steps in 
a process.
1. Write events or steps in the
order they happened.
2. Write the ﬁrst event or ﬁrst
step in the ﬁrst box.
3. Add steps or events to
the chart.
Interview
1. Write questions.
2. Record your partner’s answers.
Know/Want to Know/
Learned Chart (KWL)
1. Write the topic in the top box.
2. Write things you know on the 
left.
3. Write things you want to know 
in the middle.
4. Write things you learned
on the right.
Matrix ( 4 x 4 , 5 x 5, 6 x 6)
Use these cha rts to take notes, 
organize information, and compare 
items. You can also use these 
cha r ts to take notes during group 
work or to plan and play games. 
1. Write the topics in the top row.
2. Add to the chart as you read 
or write.
Note-taking chart 
Use a note-taking chart to take 
notes on readings and during 
group work. 
1. Write the topic at the top 
of the page.
2. Take notes on main ideas, 
details, or other important 
information.
1Our World
© National Geographic Learning, a part of Cengage.
Pie charts
Use pie charts to show numbers 
and parts of a whole. 
1. Put items into groups. 
2. Find out how many items or 
people go in each group. 
3. Add sections to the chart to 
show the number in each 
group. 
Spider map
Use a Spider map to help plan 
your writing, or to list main ideas 
and details of a reading. 
1. Write the topic in the circle.
2. Write the main ideas on the 
lines connected to the circle.
3. Add details about the main 
idea on the smaller lines.
Storyboard
Use a Storyboard to predict or 
show sequence of events.
1. Write the most important steps 
or events.
2. Put the events in the order 
they happen.
3. Draw picture if you want.
Sunshine organizer
Use a Sunshine organizer to 
help you answer questions 
about a topic.
1. Write the topic in the circle.
2. Write questions and answers 
next to the triangles.
T-chart
Use a T-chart to take notes or 
compare two items.
1. Write the topics in the top 
two boxes. For example, write 
Cause on the left and Effect
on the right.
2. Add to the chart as you read 
or write.
Three-column chart 
Two-column Chart
Use these charts to take notes, 
organize information, and plan 
your writing. 
1. Write the topics in the top row. 
2. Add to the chart as you read 
or write. 
2Our World
Copyright © National Geographic Learning, a part of Cengage Learning.
274

1Our World
Copyright © National Geographic Learning, a part of Cengage Learning.
Pie charts
Use pie charts to show numbers 
and parts of a whole. 
1. Put items into groups.
2. 
Find out how many items or 
people go in each group.
3. Add sections to the chart to 
show the number in each 
group.
Spider map
Use a Spider map to help plan 
your writing, or to list main ideas 
and details of a reading. 
1. Write the topic in the circle.
2. 
Write the main ideas on the 
lines connected to the circle.
3. Add details about the main 
idea on the smaller lines.
Storyboard
Use a Storyboard to predict or 
show sequence of events.
1. Write the most important steps 
or events.
2. Put the events in the order they 
happen.
3. Draw pictures if you want.
Sunshine organizer
Use a Sunshine organizer to 
help you answer questions 
about a topic.
1. Write the topic in the circle.
2. Write questions and answers 
next to the triangles.
T-chart
Use a T-chart to take notes or 
compare two items.
1. Write the topics in the top 
two boxes. For example, write 
Cause on the left and Effect 
on the right.
2. Add to the chart as you read 
or write.
Three-column chart 
Two-column Chart
Use these charts to take notes, 
organize information, and plan 
your writing. 
1. Write the topics in the top row.
2. Add to the chart as you read 
or write.
2Our World
© National Geographic Learning, a part of Cengage.
275

Timeline
Use a timeline to show 
the sequence of events.
1. Write events in the order they 
happened.
2. Write the ﬁrst event and date 
on the left or at the top.
3. Put the last event and date on 
the right or at the bottom.
Tree 1, 2, 3
Use a tree to organize ideas or 
ﬁnd solutions to a problem. 
1. Write the topic or problem.
2. Add details or solutions to 
the problem.
Venn diagram (2 circles)
Venn diagram (3 circles)
Use a Venn diagram to compare 
and contrast two or three things.
1. Write the things you are 
comparing in the circles.
2. Write ways the things
are different.
3. When the circles meet, write 
ways the things are the same.
Vocabulary log
Use a vocabulary log to 
remember new words.
1. Write new words in the log.
2. Take notes on the wo rds or add 
e xam p le sente n ces from th e unit .
3. Draw pictures if you want.
4. Look at your log to study the 
new words.
Word web
Use a word web to build 
vocabulary or to show the 
main idea and details.
1. Write the vocabulary word or 
main idea in the large circle in 
the middle.
2. Write other vocabulary words 
and details in the smaller circles.
3. Add more circles as needed.
3Our World
© National Geographic Learning, a part of Cengage Learning.
276
